intro: Python abstracts away memory management and system architecture to optimize for developer velocity and rapid prototyping. Transitioning to C shifts the focus to bare-metal performance, hardware access, and manual memory control, which is essential for systems programming or embedded applications. Developers typically make this leap when Python's runtime overhead and global interpreter lock (GIL) become unacceptable bottlenecks for computational tasks.

diffs.from: Automatic reference counting and cyclic garbage collection.
diffs.to: Manual explicit memory allocation and deallocation (malloc/free).

diffs.from: Interpreted bytecode executed via a runtime VM (e.g., CPython).
diffs.to: Ahead-of-time (AOT) compiled directly to native machine code.

diffs.from: Cooperative/preemptive threading constrained by the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL).
diffs.to: Native OS threads (pthreads) or hardware-level parallel execution.

diffs.from: Batteries-included with high-level data structures and modules.
diffs.to: Minimalist ISO standard library focused on low-level I/O and string operations.

examples.title: File Reading
examples.description: Reading a text file line-by-line and handling potential errors safely.

examples.title: List / Array Squaring
examples.description: Iterating over a collection, transforming elements, and populating a new sequence.
examples.fromCode:
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
squared = [x ** 2 for x in numbers]

tips.title: Manage Allocations Manually
tips.body: Always match every malloc or calloc invocation with a corresponding free to prevent memory leaks.

tips.title: Enforce Array Boundaries
tips.body: C does not perform runtime bounds checking; you must track array lengths manually to avoid buffer overflows.

tips.title: Replace Objects with Structs
tips.body: Convert Python classes into C structs and implement functions that accept pointers to these structs as instances.

tips.title: Handle String Null Terminators
tips.body: Remember that strings in C are character arrays terminated by a null byte (\0), requiring an extra byte of space.
